answers.value:
Our mortgage account has fallen into arrears over the past 8 months due to a combination of reduced household income, unexpected medical expenses, and increased cost of living pressures.

In May of last year, I (Daniel) was moved from a full bonus-eligible role to a reduced bonus structure as part of a company-wide cost-cutting initiative. Historically, my annual performance bonus made up roughly 15–20% of my total income and we had been using that bonus to cover a portion of our annual expenses such as school fees, car insurance, and home maintenance. When the bonus was reduced significantly, we initially tried to absorb the shortfall through cutting discretionary spending, but over time it became clear that our budget was much tighter than we had planned when we first took out the mortgage.

On top of this, in August our younger daughter, Sophie (age 6), was diagnosed with asthma. While it is being managed, we had several months of higher-than-expected medical costs including specialist consultations, additional GP visits, and paying out-of-pocket for certain inhalers and equipment that were not fully covered under our standard health plan. These medical expenses tended to cluster around the same time as our back-to-school costs (uniforms, books, and activity fees for both children), which put a lot of pressure on our cash flow in late summer and early autumn.

Around the same period, our fixed-rate electricity and gas contracts expired and we were moved onto a higher tariff before we were able to switch providers. Combined with general increases in groceries and fuel, our monthly outgoings rose by approximately 250–300 EUR compared with the previous year. We did not immediately adjust our standing orders or our spending habits sufficiently to reflect this change, and we ended up relying on our overdraft and credit cards to bridge the gap. This, in turn, led to higher interest charges and minimum payments on those credit lines.

As a result, we began to fall behind on the full mortgage repayment amounts. Initially, we missed the equivalent of about half a payment when we prioritized paying a medical bill for Sophie and a car repair that could not be delayed (the car is needed for Emily to get to the school where she teaches, which is not directly served by public transport). We thought we would catch up the following month, but with each subsequent month, the combination of ongoing higher living costs and existing arrears made it increasingly difficult to return to a fully current position.

We also made a budgeting mistake in assuming that Emily would be able to increase her hours from part-time to nearly full-time during the current academic year. However, the school did not receive the expected increase in student numbers, so the additional hours we had anticipated did not materialize. This meant that her income remained lower than we had projected when we planned our annual budget, and that gap directly contributed to our difficulty in keeping up with the scheduled mortgage repayments.

To address the situation, over the last three months we have taken several concrete steps: we have created a detailed household budget and tracked all our spending; we cancelled non-essential subscriptions (such as multiple streaming services and a gym membership I was rarely using); we renegotiated our broadband and mobile contracts to lower tariffs; and we have set up a small emergency fund so that unavoidable irregular expenses (like car servicing or school trips) do not immediately force us into choosing between the mortgage and other commitments. I have also taken on some additional freelance project work in the evenings for a former employer, which is starting to generate a modest but steady extra income stream.

Despite these efforts, the arrears that accumulated during the worst months have left us behind on the mortgage, and the current full repayment amount is still challenging for us to meet consistently while we clear the backlog of other short-term debts and stabilize our finances. We are seeking this review in the hope of agreeing a more sustainable repayment arrangement—potentially a temporary reduction or restructuring—that would allow us to regularize the account, protect our family home, and gradually clear the arrears over a realistic timeframe while continuing to meet our essential living expenses and obligations to our children.
